hook: An Aztec wind-god shrine stranded in a metro interchange
facts: Unearthed in 1967 during construction of the Pino Suarez metro station
Circular base honours Ehecatl, wind aspect of Quetzalcoatl
Often called the smallest archaeological zone in Mexico
Sits in the open-air passage between Metro lines 1 and 2
w: Digging the metro in 1967, crews hit a round stone platform and, instead of pouring concrete over it, built the station around it. Commuters now stream past a shrine to Ehecatl, the wind aspect of Quetzalcoatl, its curve shaped to a coiled serpent. Tenochtitlan surfacing between turnstiles.

hook: A dozen naturally mummified bodies in a Carmelite crypt
facts: Housed beneath the 17th-century ex-convent, now Museo de El Carmen
Twelve bodies, mostly donors to the colonial San Angelo Martir college
Found by Zapatista troops during the Revolution
Placed in glass caskets in 1929; crypt restored and reopened 2012
w: Zapatista soldiers poking through the abandoned convent during the Revolution found the dry crypt air had turned a dozen buried donors to leather. Carmelite monks got dug up after seven years; benefactors stayed, and stayed, and slowly mummified. Now they lie in glass cases under San Angel. Eternity by accident.

hook: A 1620s chamber where whispers cross diagonally
facts: Built around 1620-26, attributed to friar-architect Andres de San Miguel
Stood amid the Carmelite orchards that became Chimalistac
Dedicated to the Virgin of Sorrows
Whisper in one corner; only the diagonal corner hears it
legend: Said to have let silent Carmelite monks hold whispered conversations
w: A small vaulted chamber from the 1620s on a cobbled Chimalistac lane, all that survives of the Carmelites' vast orchards. Stand in one corner facing the wall and whisper; only the person in the opposite corner hears you. Legend says it let monks sworn to silence murmur across the room. Acoustics as loophole.

hook: One man's million toys crammed into a graffiti-clad walkup
facts: Founded by architect Roberto Shimizu, who began collecting aged 10
Over a million pieces; roughly 40,000 on display
Four floors in a former department store in gritty Colonia Doctores
Facade and stairwells covered in commissioned street art
w: Roberto Shimizu started buying toys at ten and never stopped; his hoard tops a million pieces, with some 40,000 jammed floor to ceiling across four storeys of a former Doctores department store. Luchadores, tin robots, board games, oddities, no white walls, no order. Street art swallows the facade. Joyful chaos.

hook: A gilded cantina with Pancho Villa's bullet in the ceiling
facts: Began in 1876 as a tea and chocolate shop; cantina from 1895
Ornate Belle Epoque interior of mirrors and carved wood
Long the haunt of politicians and revolutionary generals
Staff point out a bullet hole in the plaster overhead
legend: They say Pancho Villa fired the ceiling shot on entering the capital in 1914
w: Gilt, mirrors and carved wood from 1895, the kind of cantina where politicians plotted over brandy. Look up: a hole in the plaster ceiling. They say Pancho Villa fired it in 1914 after riding into the capital, settling some quarrel with his pistol. Staff will happily point if you cannot find the bullet yourself.

hook: A Moorish iron pavilion built for a New Orleans world's fair
facts: Designed by engineer Jose Ramon Ibarrola in cast and wrought iron
Represented Mexico at the 1884-85 New Orleans World's Fair
Later moved through several sites before landing here in 1910
Centrepiece of the Alameda de Santa Maria la Ribera
w: Lacy wrought iron in horseshoe arches and a stained-glass dome, Moorish fantasy assembled to fly Mexico's flag at the 1884 New Orleans world's fair. Shipped home, it bounced between sites before settling into a neighbourhood park in 1910. Locals court, busk and nap beneath it. A pavilion that crossed an ocean to retire.

hook: A Porfirian temple of fossils with a mammoth in the hall
facts: Opened 1906 for the Instituto Geologico de Mexico
Art Nouveau building with an ornate iron-and-marble staircase
Mammoth skeleton reconstructed from a dozen different specimens
Holds minerals, meteorites and a 19th-century fossil collection
w: Behind an Art Nouveau facade of volcanic stone, a science palace from 1906, when the Porfiriato wanted to show off the nation's rocks. An iron-and-marble staircase coils up past cases of meteorites and crystals, and a mammoth, stitched from a dozen separate beasts, looms in the hall. Deep time under cast-iron flourishes.

hook: Mexico's only round 18th-century church, over a sacred spring
facts: Built 1777-91 by architect Francisco Guerrero y Torres
Only circular church surviving from 18th-century Mexico
Raised over a spring pilgrims believed had curative water
Tiled domes and an oval, undulating Baroque floor plan
legend: Pilgrims long credited the little well's water with healing wounds
w: A swirl of tiled domes and curving walls beside the Guadalupe shrine, raised over a spring whose water pilgrims swore could heal wounds. Architect Guerrero y Torres gave it an oval, rippling plan, the only round church to survive Mexico's 18th century. Baroque geometry built around a holy puddle.

hook: A Tepito chapel marking where slavery is said to have begun
facts: Plaque reads Tequipeuhcan, place where slavery began
Marks where Cuauhtemoc is held to have been captured in 1521
Franciscan hermitage rebuilt into a parish around 1699
Stands at the northern edge of the Tepito barrio
legend: Tradition places the surrender of the last Aztec emperor on this spot
w: A reddish parish church at the rough northern edge of Tepito, its facade bearing a Nahuatl word, Tequipeuhcan: place where slavery began. Tradition holds that Cuauhtemoc, the last Aztec emperor, surrendered to Cortes near here in 1521. A neighbourhood corner carrying the weight of an empire's last day.

hook: A street named for a ruined informant who sat and grieved
facts: Old name for stretches near Correo Mayor and Moneda
Tale dates to the early colonial decades after the conquest
Subject was reputedly a noble turned viceregal informant
Counted among the oldest legends of the city
legend: They say a stripped, disgraced noble sat motionless here in sorrow until he died
w: Near the old Palacio de Axayacatl, a colonial street kept a strange name for centuries. They say a fallen Aztec noble, once an informant grown rich and ruined by the viceroy, sat here head buried in his arms, motionless as a statue, until grief simply killed him. One of the city's oldest legends, carved into a corner.

n: Museo del Pulque y las Pulquerias
hook: A shrine to the agave drink above a working pulqueria
facts: Opened 2019 in the former Convento de San Hipolito
Three rooms on pulque history, myth and production
Sits directly above a busy ground-floor pulcata
Keeps an ajolote tank nodding to the agave's water world
legend: Pulque myth tells of a possum stealing the moon's brew for humankind
w: Above a roaring pulcata in a 16th-century convent, three small rooms rescuing the reputation of pulque, the milky agave brew the conquest never quite killed off. Murals retell the myth where a possum steals the moon's drink for humankind. There is even an axolotl tank. Climb the stairs sober, descend for a cup.

hook: Diego Rivera's black lava pyramid built to house his idols
facts: Designed by Diego Rivera from dark volcanic pedregal stone
Holds some 50,000 pre-Hispanic pieces he collected
Completed after his 1957 death by O'Gorman and Pagelson
Name means house surrounded by water in Nahuatl
w: Diego Rivera spent a fortune on pre-Hispanic idols, then designed a windowless black pyramid of lava rock to keep them, part Toltec temple, part personal mausoleum. He died in 1957 before it opened; friends finished it. Mosaic ceilings glow over sloping stone galleries packed with stone gods. A muralist's private underworld.
